title: Lumbee Fairness Act
stage_label: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held. Hearings printed: S.Hrg. 119-275.
plain_summary: This bill gives federal recognition to the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina. Once recognized, tribe members will be able to get the same services and benefits that other federally recognized tribes receive, like healthcare and education programs. The federal government will also be allowed to take land and hold it in trust for the tribe. North Carolina state laws will still apply to tribal lands unless the state and tribe make an agreement to change that.
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held. Hearings printed: S.Hrg. 119-275.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Lumbee Fairness Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Federal Recognition
sections.text: This section changes an older law from nineteen fifty six about the Lumbee people. The changes do several things. First, it removes one section completely. Second, it reorganizes the beginning of the old law to include findings that Congress has made about the Lumbee people. Third, it creates definitions for important terms. The term Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. The term Tribe means the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina or the Lumbee Indians of North Carolina. Fourth, it adds new sections about federal recognition. The federal government now recognizes the Lumbee Tribe as an official Indian tribe. This tribe was already listed as petitioner number sixty five by the Office of Federal Acknowledgment. All federal laws and rules that apply to Indians and Indian tribes will now apply to the Lumbee Tribe and its members. Other groups of Indians in Robeson County and nearby counties in North Carolina can still ask to be recognized as tribes if their members are not already part of the Lumbee Tribe. Fifth, it explains that the Lumbee Tribe and its members can now get all services and benefits that the federal government gives to recognized Indian tribes. Members who live in Robeson County, Cumberland County, Hoke County, and Scotland County in North Carolina will be treated as if they live on or near an Indian reservation for the purpose of getting these services. The Secretary of the Interior and the Secretary of Health and Human Services must work with the tribe to figure out what services are needed and tell Congress about these needs. The tribe must provide a verified list of its members to get these services. The Secretary of the Interior has two years to check this list to make sure it follows the membership rules in the tribe's constitution from November sixteen, two thousand and one. Sixth, it allows the Secretary of the Interior to take land and hold it in trust for the benefit of the Lumbee Tribe. Land applications in Robeson County, North Carolina will be treated the same as applications for land on existing reservations. Seventh, it explains that North Carolina will continue to have legal authority over criminal cases and civil lawsuits on Lumbee tribal land. However, the state can agree to transfer some of this authority to the federal government after consulting with the tribe. Any such transfer must wait two years after the agreement is made. This section does not change how the Indian Child Welfare Act applies.
